summary: Gruber pushes back on Mark Gurman's Bloomberg report framing Apple's AR glasses postponement as a major setback, arguing the timeline was never realistic to begin with. He notes that Gurman's own 2020 reporting suggested the mixed-reality headset might ship in 2022, making a 2023 release hardly late. Gruber compares expecting AR glasses one year after a bulky VR headset to expecting a Mars mission right after the moon landing. He's skeptical of the tethered battery reports but allows it could be a deliberate design tradeoff. He closes with optimism that Apple's headset will surprise people, just as iPhone and Apple Watch redefined their categories rather than iterating on existing products.
key_points: Gurman's framing of AR glasses being 'delayed' is misleading since Apple never publicly announced a timeline, and the technology gap between VR headsets and lightweight AR glasses is enormous
The reported tethered external battery pack could be a deliberate design decision rather than a desperate compromise, though it sounds like a non-starter on the surface
A cheaper follow-up headset after a $3,000 debut product is obvious and unremarkable news, not a scoop
Gruber disputes The Information's report about a front-facing display showing the wearer's facial expressions, saying Apple's team had joked about the idea and someone may have taken it seriously
Apple's track record suggests the headset will redefine expectations rather than be an incremental improvement on existing VR products
money_quotes: To me it's like trying to argue in 1968 that NASA's first mission to Mars had been postponed indefinitely, when they hadn't yet landed on the moon.
You don't have be an engineer to realize how much more difficult regular-looking glasses with all-day battery life, comfort, and functionality are than a bulky ski-mask-style VR headset.
It certainly shouldn't be what it sounds like in these reports, which is 'Fuck it, we need to ship something, so let's just stick the battery on a cable and it'll go in their pocket.'
A 'Man Bites Dog' story would be if Apple didn't see any need for a headset that cost less than $3,000.

In 2006 it was dogma that all cell phones were destined to suck, because the carriers sucked, the carriers controlled the phones, and it seemed like no company could ever break that control. Things can change. Progress comes.

But the iPhone was not an iPod phone. Apple Watch was not a Fitbit with a higher price. If Apple is still Apple, this first headset should be much more than a slightly nicer version of VR headsets as we know them.
Something is definitely coming from Apple from this team this year, and I get the sense the company thinks it's going to be something special.
key_insight: Reports framing Apple's AR glasses as delayed are misleading because the technology was never close to ready, and the real story is whether Apple's imminent mixed-reality headset will redefine the category the way iPhone and Apple Watch did.
